Translation: نتایج این تحقیق، که از روش افزودن استاندارد استفاده کرد، در جدول 1 نشان داده شده است. نتایج به دست آمده نشان داد که بازیابی این ترکیبات قابل قبول است و MOF-235/GO/CPE به طور پتانسیل ممکن است برای تعیین داروهای DAN و DTIC در نمونه های واقعی استفاده شود.

لطفا جملات زیر را به انگلیسی بازیابی کنید. The results of this investigation, which used the standard addition method, are shown in Table 1. The obtained results indicated that the recoveries of these compounds are acceptable and the MOF-235/GO/CPE can potentially be used to determine DAN and DTIC drugs in real samples.

The results of the investigation, which employed the standard addition method, are presented in Table 1. The achieved outcomes demonstrate that the recoveries of these compounds are acceptable, and it is possible to employ MOF-235/GO/CPE for the identification of DAN and DTIC drugs in actual samples.

For the purpose of identifying DNR as a chemotherapeutic medication, an electrochemical sensor has been created in the current study by modifying a CPE with MOF-235/GO nanocomposite. Due to the high conductivity of GO and the very strong synergy between GO and MOF-235, the combination of these materials effectively enhanced the electrocatalytic activity of modified electrode for the redox reaction of DAN.

In this study, an electrochemical sensor was developed to identify DNR as a chemotherapeutic drug. The sensor was created by modifying a CPE with MOF-235/GO nanocomposite. The incorporation of GO, known for its high conductivity, along with MOF-235, resulted in a strong synergy between these materials, significantly enhancing the electrocatalytic activity of the modified electrode for the redox reaction of DAN.

In this study, an electrochemical sensor was developed to identify DNR as a chemotherapeutic drug. The sensor was created by modifying a CPE with MOF-235/GO nanocomposite. The incorporation of GO, known for its high conductivity, along with MOF-235, resulted in a strong synergy between these materials, significantly enhancing the electrocatalytic activity of the modified electrode for the redox reaction of DAN.

این مطالعه با هدف شناسایی داروی شیمی درمانی DNR، یک حسگر الکتروشیمیایی ایجاد کرد. این حسگر با استفاده از ترکیب نانوکامپوزیت MOF-235/GO به یک CPE تغییر یافته ساخته شد. در نتیجه یک همزیستی قوی بین این مواد وجود دارد که باعث افزایش چشمگیر فعالیت الکتروکاتالیزری الکترود تغییر یافته برای واکنش اکسید-ترکیب DAN می‌شود.

The MOF-235/GO/CPE sensor, on the other hand, was employed to analyze DNR in the presence of DTIC, and when DNR and DTIC were detected simultaneously with significant potential differences, the modified electrode demonstrated exceptional electrocatalytic activity.

Under optimal conditions, an outstanding linear correlation between Ipa and DNR concentration was observed in the range of 0.005-400.0 μM, with a detection limit of ???? μM. Additionally, the MOF-235/GO/CPE sensor was utilized to analyze DNR in the presence of DTIC. When DNR and DTIC were both detected simultaneously with substantial potential differences, the modified electrode exhibited exceptional electrocatalytic activity.

It is advised that the recommended method be used as a suitable sensor for DNR and DTIC analysis in real samples because it shown excellent qualities like simplicity, good sensitivity, and quick response.

Based on its remarkable qualities such as simplicity, good sensitivity, and rapid response, it is recommended to utilize the proposed method as a suitable sensor for the analysis of DNR and DTIC in real samples.

Anthracycline antibiotics are one of the most frequently used drugs in the treatment of different types of cancer. They proved to be effective in anticancer therapy for ovarian, stomach and lung cancer as well as leukemias. Daunorubicin (DNR), which is also known as daunomycin, is one of new approved anthracycline anticancer drugs.

Anthracycline antibiotics are extensively utilized medications in the treatment of various cancer types. They have demonstrated efficacy in combating ovarian, stomach, lung cancers, as well as leukemias. Among these, Daunorubicin (DNR), also known as daunomycin, is a recently approved anthracycline anticancer drug.

Anthracyclines (i) inhibit macromolecules synthesis through intercalation into double stranded DNA structure, (ii) inhibit topoisomerase II causing the formation of DNA adducts, (iii) produce reactive oxygen species (ROS). Anthracyclines intercalate between the DNA base pairs via planar anthracycline ring or bind via interaction of amino-sugar moiety of the negatively charged phosphate groups in the DNA minor groove.

Anthracyclines exert their anticancer effects through several mechanisms. Firstly, they intercalate into the double-stranded DNA structure, inhibiting macromolecule synthesis. Additionally, they inhibit topoisomerase II, resulting in the formation of DNA adducts. Lastly, anthracyclines generate reactive oxygen species (ROS). These drugs can intercalate between DNA base pairs either via the planar anthracycline ring or by binding to the amino-sugar moiety, which interacts with the negatively charged phosphate groups in the minor groove of DNA.

Daunorubicin is prescribed for the treatment of chronic myelogenous leukemia (CML), famous acute myeloid leukemia (AML), Kaposi’s sarcoma, and acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL). Although this drug is used for chemotherapy, its many side effects such as hair loss and cardiac arrest make it necessary to control the dosage used in the stages of chemotherapy with this drug.

Daunorubicin is commonly prescribed for the treatment of chronic myelogenous leukemia (CML), acute myeloid leukemia (AML), Kaposi's sarcoma, and acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL). While it is an effective chemotherapy drug, it is associated with various side effects such as hair loss and cardiac arrest. Therefore, careful dosage control is necessary during chemotherapy stages involving daunorubicin to mitigate these adverse effects.

Anticancer drug Dacarbazine (DTIC) is a cell cycle nonspecific antineoplastic agent that functions as an alkylating agent after activation in the liver. The drug is used in the treatment of metastatic malignant melanoma, soft tissue sarcoma, neuroblastoma, Kaposi’s sarcoma, and other tumors. It is also given to patients with Hodgkin’s disease, notably with doxorubicin, bleomycin, and vinblastine.

Dacarbazine (DTIC) is an anticancer drug that acts as a cell cycle nonspecific antineoplastic agent. It functions as an alkylating agent after undergoing activation in the liver. DTIC is employed in the treatment of various conditions, including metastatic malignant melanoma, soft tissue sarcoma, neuroblastoma, Kaposi's sarcoma, and other tumors. Additionally, it is administered to patients with Hodgkin's disease, often in combination with doxorubicin, bleomycin, and vinblastine.

The investigation of the interaction between drug molecules and DNA is important for understanding the relationship between drug dosage adjustment and drug response in chemotherapeutic applications. DTIC and its metabolites show antitumor activities by alkalization. The metabolite responsible for alkalinity of DTIC is methyl diazonium ion.

The investigation of the interaction between drug molecules and DNA holds significance in comprehending the connection between drug dosage adjustment and drug response in chemotherapy. In the context of DTIC, the drug and its metabolites exhibit antitumor activities through alkalization. Specifically, the metabolite accountable for the alkalinity of DTIC is the methyl diazonium ion.

The use of DTIC at high doses has significant side effects which are vomiting and nausea. Thus, the monitoring of DTIC level in biologic samples is highly recommended for safe cure in cancer chemotraphy. Additionally, the monitoring of the plasmatic concentration of antineoplastic drugs is important for the optimization of therapy and management of side effects.

The administration of DTIC at high doses is associated with notable side effects, namely vomiting and nausea. Consequently, monitoring the level of DTIC in biological samples is strongly recommended to ensure safe and effective cancer chemotherapy. Furthermore, monitoring the plasma concentration of antineoplastic drugs is crucial for optimizing therapy and managing potential side effects.

Chemotherapy medicines like DNR and DTIC are frequently used to treat cancer. There are currently some techniques for DNR and DTIC determination, including thin layer chromatography (TCL), high performance liquid chromatography mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS), liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS) and spectrophotometery. It should be noted that these conventional techniques, despite their own advantages, have some disadvantages such as high cost, time consuming and difficulty of sample pre-treatments.

Chemotherapy medications like DNR and DTIC are commonly employed in cancer treatment. Several techniques are available for the determination of DNR and DTIC, including thin layer chromatography (TLC), high-performance liquid chromatography mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS), liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS), and spectrophotometry. However, it is important to note that these conventional techniques, despite their respective advantages, have certain drawbacks such as high cost, time consumption, and complexities associated with sample pre-treatment.

Therefore, among these, special attention has been aroused towards electrochemical approaches because of their special features like short analysis time, portability, cost-effectiveness, miniaturization capability and sensitivity.

As a result, electrochemical approaches have garnered significant attention among these techniques due to their unique characteristics, including short analysis time, portability, cost-effectiveness, miniaturization capability, and high sensitivity.

Carbon paste electrodes (CPEs) as working electrodes in the structure of electrochemical sensors have concerned by many researchers owing to weak background current, wide potential window, the possibility of mass production, and the possibility of miniaturization and surface modification.

Due to their favorable attributes such as low background current, wide potential window, potential for mass production, and compatibility with miniaturization and surface modification, carbon paste electrodes (CPEs) have attracted significant attention from researchers. They are commonly used as working electrodes in the construction of electrochemical sensors.

Modified electrodes can have useful analytical applications, including It is possible to facilitate an electrochemical reaction (reduction in excess potential), increasing the density flow, reduction of disturbances on the analyte, simultaneous measurement of several compounds in the presence of each other, increase selectivity and sensitivity towards the analyte in measurements, studying reactions with very fast kinetics, performing some impossible reactions in the desired solvent and preventing the destruction of the electrode surface and increasing its lifespan.

Modified electrodes offer various analytical advantages, including facilitating electrochemical reactions by reducing excess potential, enhancing flow density, reducing interference on the analyte, enabling simultaneous measurement of multiple compounds in their presence, increasing selectivity and sensitivity of measurements towards the analyte, studying reactions with fast kinetics, conducting certain reactions in desired solvents that would be otherwise impossible, and preventing electrode surface destruction while prolonging its lifespan.

The properties of materials as successful modifiers include high porosity, large surface area, admirable electrical conductivity, excellent stability, non-toxicity and cost-effectiveness. Nanostructure materials have been considered in recent years for electrode modification. Electrical conductivity and active surface area are improved by the addition of nanomaterials to the electrode's structure or to its surface.

Successful modifiers possess certain properties that contribute to their effectiveness, such as high porosity, large surface area, favorable electrical conductivity, excellent stability, non-toxicity, and cost-effectiveness. In recent years, nanostructured materials have gained attention for electrode modification. The incorporation of nanomaterials into the electrode's structure or onto its surface enhances electrical conductivity and active surface area. This, in turn, improves the performance of the modified electrode.

Graphite oxide (GO), formed by treating graphite with very strong oxidizing agents such as KClO3/HNO3, has a layered structure and a non-stochiometric chemical composition, which depends on the level of oxidation. Graphene has good conductivity, chemical durability and large surface area, which makes it suitable for loading and confining nanosized materials

Graphite oxide (GO) is obtained by treating graphite with potent oxidizing agents like KClO3/HNO3. It possesses a layered structure and a non-stoichiometric chemical composition that varies based on the degree of oxidation. Graphene, derived from GO, exhibits excellent conductivity, chemical durability, and a large surface area. These characteristics make it well-suited for loading and encapsulating nanosized materials.

Metal organic frameworks (MOFs) are a new kind of porous crystalline material synthesized by the assembly of metal ions and organic ligands. Metal organic frameworks (MOFs) have attracted attention over the past two decades due to their large surface area, structural diversity, high porosity, morphological features, ease of design, and pore-resizing properties.

Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs) are a class of porous crystalline materials formed by the coordination of metal ions with organic ligands. They have gained significant attention in the last two decades due to their remarkable properties. MOFs exhibit a large surface area, structural diversity, high porosity, unique morphological features, ease of design, and the ability to tune their pore sizes. These characteristics make MOFs attractive for various applications such as gas storage, catalysis, drug delivery, and sensing.

They are generally made of transition metals arranged in a network with suitable organic ligands. Compared to porous carbon materials and zeolites, MOFs are synthesized under mild conditions, and allow systematic engineering of their chemical and physical properties through modification of their components. Very recently, MOFs have been used in the electrochemical fields including electrochemical sensors, fuel cells, lithium-ion rechargeable batteries, supercapacitors, solar cells, Li–S batteries, because of their electrochemical activity.

MOFs are typically composed of transition metals coordinated with organic ligands, forming a network structure. Unlike porous carbon materials and zeolites, MOFs can be synthesized under mild conditions and offer the advantage of systematic engineering of their chemical and physical properties through component modification. Recently, MOFs have found applications in the field of electrochemistry, including electrochemical sensors, fuel cells, lithium-ion rechargeable batteries, supercapacitors, solar cells, and Li-S batteries, due to their inherent electrochemical activity and tunable properties.

However, the stability and poor electrical conductivity of traditional MOFs limit their applications. The use of graphene-based materials with rich functional groups as templates for the growth of MOF particles can not only reduce the poor conductivity and structural instability of MOFs, but also prevent the aggregation and restacking phenomenon between graphene sheets, thereby greatly expanding the application scope of MOFs. Due to the tunable structure and composition of derivatives, MOF-derived/graphene materials can also be used as multifunctional precursors, especially in the field of electrochemistry.

Indeed, the stability and low electrical conductivity of conventional MOFs impose limitations on their applications. However, by utilizing graphene-based materials with abundant functional groups as templates for MOF particle growth, it becomes possible to address these limitations. This approach not only improves the electrical conductivity and structural stability of MOFs but also prevents aggregation and restacking between graphene sheets, thereby significantly expanding the potential applications of MOFs. Moreover, MOF-derived/graphene materials exhibit tunable structures and compositions, making them versatile and valuable precursors, particularly in the field of electrochemistry.

The current work aimed to fabricate a new CPE with the combination of MOF-Co3(BTC)2/GO nanocomposite for determination of anticancer drug DNR in the presence of DTIC. The synergic effect of the MOF-Co3(BTC)2/GO nanocomposite helped improve the ability of sensor to make highly sensitive determination of DNR under optimum conditions. In addition to sensitivity, wide linear dynamic range, acceptable repeatability, high stability and good repeatability were the unique properties of the proposed sensor. The designed electrode (MOF-Co3(BTC)2/GO/CPE) showed high selectivity for determination of DNR and DTIC in real samples.

In this study, a new carbon paste electrode (CPE) was developed by incorporating a MOF-Co3(BTC)2/GO nanocomposite. The primary objective was to utilize this electrode for the determination of the anticancer drug DNR in the presence of DTIC. The synergistic effect of the MOF
